metadata.title: The Model Fit in Memory and Crashed Anyway

beats.narration_text: Salaam, Liam. This is Liam, in for Bear. 256 kilobytes of RAM, a 180-kilobyte model — simple subtraction says it fits, and it crashes 40 milliseconds into the first inference.

props.command: My model is 180 KB and I have 256 KB of SRAM — why does it crash on the first inference?
output: Weights fit in flash. Activations fill SRAM at runtime — peak, not size, is the constraint.

beats.narration_text: From the book: the STM32H743 SRAM budget after one arena bump — you can see exactly where inference overflows the one-megabyte wall and the system crashes.

The naive count: 180 kilobytes of weights plus 60 kilobytes of activations equals 240 kilobytes — leaving 16 kilobytes for firmware. Looks fine on paper. But the weights live in flash. The 256 kilobytes of SRAM is for runtime data only — and that's where the crash is.

With naive allocation, every layer allocates its activation buffer and holds it until inference completes. Layer 1 takes 30 kilobytes, layer 2 adds 40, layer 3 adds 50, layer 4 adds 30 — peak SRAM reaches 150 kilobytes of activations alone, before firmware stack and heap. That's the crash.

With buffer reuse, a layer's input buffer is freed the moment its output is written. Only the buffers live at one time count toward peak. Layer 1 in, layer 1 out, buffer recycled. Peak activation RAM drops from 150 kilobytes to 60 — the model runs.

The architecture that works: weights live in flash — read-only, they can be larger than SRAM. Activations live in SRAM — writable, they must fit within it. TensorFlow Lite Micro's arena allocator manages this automatically. The constraint is always peak activation RAM, not parameter count.

Your turn. Paste this into Claude: My model has n-layers layers with these activation sizes in kilobytes: list your sizes. Walk me through: one, naive peak RAM allocation, where buffers are allocated simultaneously; two, reuse allocation, only buffers alive at one time; three, how TensorFlow Lite Micro's arena allocator handles this; four, how to verify the peak before flashing. Swap in your real layer sizes and get the exact numbers before you flash.

props.command: My model has [n_layers] layers with these activation sizes: [list of sizes in KB]. Walk me through: (1) naive peak RAM allocation (buffers allocated simultaneously), (2) reuse allocation (only buffers alive at one time), (3) how TensorFlow Lite Micro's arena allocator handles this, and (4) how to verify the peak before flashing.

props.title: The Model Fit in Memory and Crashed Anyway
props.subline: embedded ai · liam, in for bear
